title: THE USE OF BEHAVIOR CHAINING IN DEVELOPING SELF‑CARE SKILLS IN CHILDREN WITH AUTISM SPECTRUM DISORDER
author: O.V. Zavalishina, Zh.Ak. Abenova
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: The article examines the theoretical and practical foundations of using the behavior chaining method in the development of self‑care skills in children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). Daily living skills such as handwashing, toothbrushing, dressing, and toileting are essential for a child’s safety, independence, participation in inclusive education, and quality of life. However, children with ASD often experience significant difficulties in acquiring these skills due to impairments in communication, imitation, motor planning, sensory processing, and behavioral regulation. The article is based on an analysis of contemporary psychological, pedagogical, and evidence‑based sources related to Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) and adaptive skill instruction. The study focused on behavior chaining as a structured sequence of interrelated actions in which each step establishes the conditions for the next and culminates in reinforcement. The main chaining procedures - forward chaining, backward chaining, and total task chaining - are described, along with criteria for selecting the most appropriate procedure depending on the learner’s baseline skills, motivation, sensory barriers, and access to natural reinforcement. Key methodological conditions for effective instruction are discussed, including functional assessment, operational definition of steps, systematic prompting and fading, reinforcement planning, error prevention, data collection, maintenance, and generalization across settings, people, and materials. Practical examples of teaching handwashing, toothbrushing, dressing, and toileting skills are provided. The article concludes that behavior chaining is a scientifically grounded and practice‑oriented method that enables complex self‑care skills to be transformed into teachable sequences of actions and effectively implemented through coordinated collaboration among professionals and parents, with respect for the child’s dignity, autonomy, and individual needs.
